steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Main Idea question. The passage flags “putatively” to signal skepticism: Smith used the invisible hand metaphor only once and narrowly (about mercantilism), and it was ignored until twentieth-century economists strategically elevated it to support their own views. The main idea must capture both Smith’s limited use and the later economists’ self-serving promotion.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘economists in the twentieth century came to realize that the metaphor was a robust model that anticipated their own views’: mischaracterizes the economists’ motivation as genuine discovery — the passage implies they promoted the metaphor to secure intellectual credentials, not because they found it independently compelling. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘it is nevertheless a useful model of how individuals produce aggregate benefits’: adds unsupported endorsement of the metaphor’s usefulness — the passage neither confirms nor denies its utility; it only questions how its fame was constructed. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘it was intended as a subtle critique of the economic theory of mercantilism’: misreads Smith’s purpose — the passage says Smith used the metaphor to make a narrow point about mercantilism, not that it was a critique of it; and this sidesteps the central point about later promotion. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The reputation of Smith’s metaphor is not due to the importance of the metaphor in Smith’s work but rather to the promotion of the metaphor by some later economists for their own ends.’: directly captures both halves of the argument: Smith used it sparingly, and the metaphor’s fame came from later self-interested promotion. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
